Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and focus, but then something would come up or I'd just feel too anxious to start. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because I was stressed about falling behind. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't seem to get myself together.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and panic-study for a few hours. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Regular schedules just never stuck for me no matter how many times I tried to set one up.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in. I'd get an email notification about something due or see a classmate mention an assignment, and I'd suddenly remember I needed to check what was happening. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just scrolling around without really engaging with anything.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a week or two where I was logging in more regularly because of those gamified tasks they introduced, which actually made it feel less intimidating. But that didn't last long because my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the whole platform again. It never became automatic for me.
Q5: I actually found the discussion posts somewhat helpful because I learn better by hearing other people's perspectives and explanations. When classmates would break down concepts in their own words, it clicked better than reading dense materials. The peer interactions were probably the most useful thing for me when I actually participated in them.
Q6: I mostly ignored the longer reading materials and the videos that went on forever. With my anxiety, sitting through a 50-minute video felt impossible, and I don't have the resources to print everything out to read offline comfortably. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The stuff that required sustained focus just got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was interactive or if I could see immediate results. The gamified tasks actually grabbed my attention because there was something satisfying about completing small challenges. If something felt like it would take forever with no clear payoff, I'd lose interest before I even started.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a trap almost. I knew I should do it to understand better, but my brain would say 'it's optional, skip it' because I was already drowning with the required work. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which sometimes was the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture notes or PDFs and try to study from my phone when I wasn't near my laptop. I also listened to study podcasts and YouTube explanations related to the course topics because hearing information works better for me. A lot of my actual learning happened through audio stuff that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd be lying in bed listening to explanations of concepts on YouTube or discussing course material with a friend over the phone. None of that shows up in any log. I'd also sometimes just think through problems in my head during my commute or while doing other things. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual thinking.
Q11: Yeah, I'd download something once, maybe skim it on the platform, then close out and try to study it later on my phone. With limited resources, I couldn't always be online, so I'd save things and access them when I had time. The system probably thought I only looked at it for a minute when really I spent way longer with it offline.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when my anxiety got really bad. I'd feel paralyzed looking at all the work I'd missed, and instead of catching up, I'd just shut down completely. Financial stress at home didn't help either. When everything piles up, the course became just another source of dread instead of something I could manage.
Q13: Absolutely. I was juggling a lot with limited resources, and some weeks I was just too exhausted or stressed to even think about coursework. When I was confused about material, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help because reaching out felt overwhelming. The fatigue was real, and it definitely showed in how little I used the LMS.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd log in, click around, maybe watch a few minutes of a video, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out because of stress or anxiety.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important, but the anxiety around starting it was so intense that I'd just avoid it. I'd convince myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. It wasn't that I didn't care; I cared too much in a way that made me freeze up instead of act.
Q16: Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then scramble to submit something. The pressure of a deadline would temporarily override my anxiety, but it meant everything was rushed and low quality. It's a terrible cycle I couldn't break out of.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that course section entirely. I wanted to improve, but my resilience was pretty shot, so feedback felt more threatening than helpful a lot of the time.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming. Having 18 online courses meant I could never really focus on any one thing properly. The way materials were organized in the LMS made it hard to know where to start when you're already behind. A clearer, simpler structure might have helped me stay on track better.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made things easier for a while because they broke tasks into smaller, less scary pieces. But overall, the sheer volume of content and the way everything was spread across different sections made it harder. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize, especially when I was already struggling to keep up.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in a lot, click around, interact with peers in discussions, but when it came to actually submitting work, I'd freeze. The anxiety of being judged on assignments was way worse than just browsing content. High activity doesn't mean you're actually producing anything; sometimes it's just anxious checking.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had low activity AND poor performance. But I can see how someone who studies efficiently offline could pull that off. If you're good at learning independently and just need the materials once, the LMS wouldn't show much. I wish I could be that person, but my situation was different.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked and for how long, but they can't show if you were actually focused or just staring at the screen. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. They definitely can't capture anxiety or the reasons behind avoidance behaviors.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting effort into understanding it, not just going through motions. It's when the material matters to you and you're mentally present. For me, true engagement was rare this semester because anxiety kept pulling me away even when I wanted to be involved.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually wanted to succeed but couldn't get past my own mental barriers. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent stressed about the course without actually working on it, or the learning I did through audio and conversations. My engagement story is way more complicated than any data could show.
